mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Colombia on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Colombia is at the very top of the continent, and it is the only South American country with coasts on both the Pacific AND the Caribbean 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Colombia?
mapQuestions.answer: Bogotá, high up in the Andes at 2,640 metres. One of the highest capital cities in the world.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Colombia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish. Colombian Spanish is famously clear and slow, and is often called the easiest Spanish to understand for new learners.

mapQuestions.question: Which crop is Colombia world-famous for?
mapQuestions.answer: Coffee. Colombia is the third-biggest coffee producer in the world. The Eje Cafetero (coffee triangle) in the Andean foothills is where most of it is grown.

mapQuestions.question: How many countries does Colombia border?
mapQuestions.answer: 5, Venezuela, Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, and Panama. Colombia is the only South American country that touches Central America.

foods.name: Arepa
foods.description: A flat grilled or fried cornmeal cake, eaten at breakfast with cheese, butter or scrambled egg, and as a side at every other meal. The arepa is to Colombia what bread is to France.

foods.name: Bandeja paisa
foods.description: The "country platter" from Medellín: rice, red beans, fried plantain, chorizo, fried egg, avocado, arepa, pork crackling, ground beef. Built for a farmer's lunch, but a tourist favourite. Easily enough for two.

foods.name: Ceviche colombiano
foods.description: Colombian-style shrimp ceviche, mixed with tomato sauce, lime, onion and coriander, served chilled with saltine crackers. Different from Peru's, and brilliant on a hot Caribbean afternoon.

foods.name: Empanada
foods.description: A bright yellow corn pastry stuffed with potato and beef, deep fried until crisp, eaten with fiery ají sauce. Sold at every bakery and snack bar.

foods.name: Tamal
foods.description: Corn dough stuffed with chicken, pork and vegetables, wrapped in a banana leaf and steamed for hours. Sunday morning breakfast in many Colombian homes.

foods.name: Chocolate con queso
foods.description: Hot chocolate with a chunk of mild salty cheese dropped right into it. The cheese half-melts and you fish it out with a spoon. Sounds wrong, tastes amazing. A Colombian breakfast.

scavengerItems.label: A "tinto" (tiny cup of black coffee) sold from a thermos on the street
Bright colonial buildings painted yellow, blue and red (Cartagena old town)
People dancing salsa or cumbia in a square, café or bar
A wax palm, Colombia's national tree and the tallest palm in the world (up to 60 m)
A Colombia national football jersey (or a James Rodríguez shirt)
A truck loaded sky-high with bananas, plantain or sugarcane
A snow-capped Andean peak (in clear weather, visible from Bogotá!)
An ox cart or a horse in the countryside
A real toucan, scarlet macaw, or hummingbird (Colombia has more bird species than any country)
A Medellín cable-car running up the hills (Metrocable) or street art in Comuna 13
A coca leaf for sale legally (traditional Andean tea, chewed for thousands of years, NOT cocaine)
A wooden Pacific-coast fishing boat or river canoe

geoMatches.description: The capital, 2,640 m up, big and modern, with the Monserrate mountain rising right behind it. Cool weather all year, even though it sits almost on the equator.
The "city of eternal spring". Spring-like weather all year, cable-car metro lines climbing into the hills, world-famous street art, and one of the most positive transformation stories of any city.
Caribbean coast walled colonial city. Brightly painted houses, horse carriages, sea breezes, and reggaeton playing from every speaker.
The Eje Cafetero in the Andean foothills. Green rolling coffee farms (fincas), the spectacular Cocora valley full of giant wax palms, and friendly small towns like Salento and Filandia.
A Colombian Caribbean island closer to Nicaragua than to mainland Colombia. Famous for its "seven-colour sea", with snorkelling on shallow coral reefs.
Colombia's window onto the Amazon, deep in the south. Three countries meet here on the river: Colombia, Brazil, and Peru. You can walk between them.

senses.smell: Fresh-roasted coffee from a tinto cart, sweet plantain frying in oil, salty Caribbean spray in Cartagena, eucalyptus on a misty Andean morning, hot ají sauce on the side of a plate…
senses.hear: Salsa and cumbia playing out of every café and shop, fast cheerful Colombian Spanish (the clearest in Latin America!), "¡Hola parce!", parrots in the trees, motorbikes weaving through traffic, vendors singing prices in markets…
senses.taste: Smooth thick sweet coffee (no milk, that's a "tinto"), warm soft arepas, melted cheese in hot chocolate, sweet ripe plantain, fresh tropical fruit you have never heard of (lulo, guanábana, mamoncillo)…
senses.feel: Cool morning chill high up in Bogotá, hot Caribbean humidity in Cartagena, the gentle bounce of a cable-car cabin gliding over Medellín, soft coffee leaves in a finca, sticky tropical sweat by the coast…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Colombia world-famous for coffee?
stories.body: Coffee was first brought to Colombia in the 1700s by Jesuit priests, but it really took off in the late 1800s when small family farmers in the Andean foothills realised the soil, the altitude and the climate were almost perfect for it. Today Colombia is the world's third-largest coffee producer (after Brazil and Vietnam) and the largest grower of fine "arabica" beans. The famous Eje Cafetero region was even given UNESCO World Heritage status in 2011 for its landscape and farming tradition. The fictional character Juan Valdez, an old farmer with a moustache, a poncho and a mule, has been Colombia's coffee mascot since 1958.

stories.question: How tall is the tallest palm on Earth?
stories.body: The wax palm (Ceroxylon quindiuense) is the national tree of Colombia, and the tallest palm tree in the world. Some grow over 60 metres tall, which is taller than a 15-storey building. They only grow in the high Andean cloud forests of Colombia and Peru. The wax palm is sacred to many Colombians, and cutting one down is illegal, historically people used to cut them on Palm Sunday for ceremonies, but the species nearly went extinct, so the church changed the rules to protect them. The most spectacular place to see them is the Cocora valley near Salento, where they tower out of green hills like long thin telephone poles with a feather on top.

stories.question: Why is Cartagena ringed by giant walls?
stories.body: When the Spanish ships started taking gold and silver from South America back to Europe in the 1500s, Cartagena was the main port they shipped it from. That made the city a magnet for pirates. After being attacked again and again, the Spanish built a huge ring of stone walls around the old town, plus a giant fort on a hill, San Felipe de Barajas. The walls are over 11 km long in total, up to 20 metres thick in places, and took nearly 200 years to finish. Today the old walled city is a UNESCO World Heritage site, and the brightly painted houses inside, the horse carriages and the bougainvillea make it one of the most beautiful cities in the Americas.

stories.question: How did Medellín transform itself?
stories.body: Thirty years ago, Medellín had one of the hardest reputations of any city in the world because of the drug trade. Today it is one of the most celebrated examples of city transformation anywhere. The government built free public libraries in the toughest hillside neighbourhoods, ran cable-cars (the "Metrocable") so the people in the hills could ride down to the city in minutes instead of hours, and built a giant outdoor escalator up the steep streets of Comuna 13. Artists turned blank walls into the most ambitious street-art project in Latin America. Medellín now wins international "innovation city" awards. The people are proud, and they want visitors to see how far the city has come.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Sometimes a place is famous for one thing, but it has changed completely. How can travellers help tell the new story instead of the old one?

stories.question: Who is Gabriel García Márquez?
stories.body: Gabriel García Márquez (called "Gabo" by Colombians) was a writer from a small town called Aracataca, on Colombia's Caribbean coast. He won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1982 for inventing a style called "magical realism", stories where impossible things (yellow butterflies following someone, a girl rising into the sky on a sheet, rain that lasts five years) happen alongside completely normal life, with everyone treating them as normal. His most famous book, "One Hundred Years of Solitude" (Cien Años de Soledad), tells the story of seven generations of one family in a fictional Colombian village called Macondo. It has been translated into 49 languages and is one of the most loved novels of the 20th century.

convoQuestions.question: Best food you have eaten in Colombia so far?
Have you said "¡Hola parce!" to anyone? Did it work?
Mountains, Caribbean coast, coffee farms, jungle, which has been the most "Colombia"?
Have you noticed how people's mood changes between cool Bogotá and hot Cartagena?
Colombia has more bird species than any country on Earth. How many have we spotted so far?
Medellín is famous for completely rebuilding itself. What do you think a city needs to change like that?

wordSearch.hint: Colombian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Toucan
animals.description: Large beaks, colourful feathers, common in the lowland forests of the Amazon and Pacific.

animals.name: Hummingbird
animals.description: Colombia has more bird species than any country, including 165+ kinds of hummingbird. They are everywhere with flowers.

animals.name: Blue morpho butterfly
animals.description: Bright electric-blue wings as big as your hand, flashing through the rainforest.

animals.name: Cotton-top tamarin
animals.description: Tiny monkeys with a wild white head-tuft, only found in north-west Colombia. Critically endangered.

animals.name: Pink river dolphin
animals.description: In the Colombian Amazon around Leticia.

animals.name: Andean condor
animals.description: National bird, found high in the Andes near the snow line.
